sentences.text: Right.

This-- well, the Germans were coming in, and they were looking for laborers, you know, to do all kinds of work with something that wasn't even necessary just-- just to-- to make it hard for you, a most disgraceful thing of [INAUDIBLE] things to do, which was just to humiliate you.
Let's put it this way.
To you humiliate you, is the right word.
And I remember something which will haunt me the rest of my life.
When they start shooting at us with guns, having it-- their German Lugers.
I remember.
I have seen them so many times.
And they were knocking on doors, and holding it on men and kicking him.
And the wife was running after him.
She was start crying.
And it so happened that this woman was pregnant.
She must have been in her late months, because it was very obvious to see-- six, seven, and eight months.
And I remember she fell over.
And he started jumping on her belly [SIGHING] and in such a horrifying way.
I remember like this-- that her breast was soaked in blood-- soaked in blood.
And he-- he kicked her again and again.
She fell over.
And then he took out his gun.
I remember like-- I even remember the click of that holster.
I still hear that click.
And that second click was-- he took out his gun, and he went over to her belly, and he shot her a number of times.
[CRYING] It was the most horrifying scene that-- I'll be honest with you.
I don't think there are words in the dictionary that you can-- [SIGHING] that you can express [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] this horrible scene.
It was most horrifying.
And it was soaked in blood.
[CRYING] [SOBBING] It was terrible.
[PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] Indescribable-- I mean, it is just impossible to tell you this.
And I have to add [INAUDIBLE] that this has-- this has been more than-- close to 50 years or more.
I see that picture day in and day out.
It's so ingrained in my mind.
I see her face.
And I have to say this.
I would pick the German out.
If he would be lined up today, [CRYING] I would pick him out today.
[PAUSES FOR 27 SECONDS]
Yes.
I was forced to work, yes.
But forced to work, of course, without any payment, because we worked outside the ghetto.
And we were trying to get sometimes some work close to some farms.
And we were trying to bring in a few potatoes, a little bit of vegetables, or some other things.
And of course, going back to the ghetto, there were the Jewish police.
And there were the Germans.
And they would frisk you, and they would find.
Some of them, they would kill.
And some of them was probably you'd get a few kicks, and they'll take away what you have-- what you have tried to brought in to some bit of food, that you shouldn't starve.
It was the most horrifying of things.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Terrible.
There was no water, and there was darkness.
It was horrifying things.
The ghetto is this separate chapter to-- to-- I wish I could relate it to you, to be able to tell you what they went through before they met their final destiny.
From Warsaw, I smuggled myself back to Malawa, to the city, where each family went on their own.
First, my mother went there back.
And then I.
Because it was very dangerous to travel on the train, so most of the time, we went by night, by foot.
And sometimes, a little by train.
Before the train come to the final station, you jumped off, not to be caught.
It was a horrifying journey to go back there to the-- And then we went back.
When I came back to Malawa, there was a ghetto-- a ghetto in Malawa too.
There were many Jews from the little shtetls, cities, around, surrounding Malawa.
But the main purpose of the Germans was to get the Jews in one particular place, to gather them up together.
That was their purpose, actually.
It was most horrifying of things.
It was terrible.
It was-- you have to realize [INAUDIBLE] Malawa was a very nice city.
And they took only maybe two or three streets.
And they forced in thousands.
Let me put it you this way.
Like, a house-- well, most in a European shtetl, was-- it was not many-- what do you call it-- floors.
Each one lived in their own cottage, if you call it now.
And they had their own little home in some of those houses.
I remember my grandparents.
There was so many people at night, there was nowhere even to lie down at night on the floor.
There were 10, 15 people, 20 people, in a house which had three, four rooms-- most horrifying thing.
And-- and the distress and the horror among the people, alone.
You had nothing to change.
You wore the same dress and the same thing.
It was the most-- it wasn't only physical, it was mental stress.
And some of them actually lost their minds.
And they were going and screaming in the street.
They couldn't believe it.
But this humiliation was the most horrendous.
We lived in my grandparents' house.
I remember even the street.
The street was Plocka, P-L-O-C-K-A.
And the number was 30.
I remember every part of it there.
There were some houses in the back yard.
And then the front house.
And I remember the garden, and I remember the old thing was a beautiful home.
I remember the [INAUDIBLE].
I remember all the things, which was, you know, as I mentioned to you before, you know every stone, and every little tree, and every little-- you know, it was a part of you.
It was ingrained in your lifetime.
Until '42.
We were finally-- in '42, they start transporting to the concentration camps.
And my family-- each one was trying to beat the odds, that we divided in three different-- there were so many transports going.
We didn't know absolutely nothing, because the-- the horrifying thing was that even when somebody came back, already or succeeded in telling what was going on there, people didn't want to believe it.
They couldn't understand.
They were saying, what [INAUDIBLE] they're just killing people or gassing people?
This man is out of his mind.
They couldn't believe it.
Nobody wanted to accept it, that something like this happened.
And many came back and some of the Polish neighbors said, you know, where you're going to is your final destiny.
You're not going to come back from there.
They didn't want to believe it, what was going on there.
They used to call it the very fancy name of 'resettlement,' that you will be looked after there, and you will go to work, and the children will be looked after.
And of course, their lies and the distortions were so--
That some of them said maybe.
And some-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there was-- to be truthfully speaking, even those who didn't believe it, there was nowhere to hide.
You couldn't run.
Even if you run away after the ghetto, most of the Polacks-- the Germans gave them, that you will become, like-- what do you call it-- two pounds of sugar if you will find a Jew there.
There was nowhere to hide.
There was nowhere to run.
And you have to realize that the starvation, and [INAUDIBLE] with the ghetto, you were-- you're [? worse than ?] half-human.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The degradation, the horror of it-- but it was the most horrifying of things.
And then we had to start transporting to-- to Auschwitz.
Well, the transport was horrifying [INAUDIBLE] things.
I remember we was, maybe, 3,000 or 4,000 in our transport.
And we were forced, by foot, to go to the station, surrounded by Germans with vicious dogs.
And the horrifying thing they said, that each one was-- shall take the most valuable things with you.
So people took the most valuable things.
And they said, make sure your name is there and your address is there.
So everyone-- so older people were carrying rucksacks with all their belongings.
[INAUDIBLE] that maybe needed-- jewelry or from the home, silverware, everything that you could carry, because you would need it.
And some have to carry some [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] even utensils.
[INAUDIBLE] think you will need it for this, or for the children this.
I remember the journey to Auschwitz was the most horrifying thing.
It took maybe about two days.
And my sister had a little boy, Yehuda.
He was bitterly crying.
There was no water, no nothing.
There were-- on the trains, there were just not enough places to sit.
It was the most horrifying journey.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [DRINKS] No facilities as a human being to-- you needed.
It was the most horror.
I didn't believe it.
[PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS]
Well, there were mixed feelings.
To be truthful, [INAUDIBLE] you had such a fight with yourself of survival.
You were so humiliated.
You didn't wash, nothing to eat.
Your body was claiming it-- the nourishment, the sleep, the medical care.
And then suddenly, you were reduced like an animal in-- in the forest.
And there was horror in that train.
Each one was fighting.
Well, let's put it this way-- the survival of the fittest.
And there was horror on that train.
It was a terrible thing.
You didn't think what's going to happen in a week or two.
You had to fight with yourself right now.
There was not enough that your mind would start, look what would happen there.
I remember we arrived in Auschwitz in the middle of night.
That was specially made at night.
And the trains were opened.
And they were screaming it-- arouse.
You know, go out from those trains.
And there were no-- it did not come in to a platform which is parallel with the train.
It was up much higher.
So people started to jump out from there.
And those which couldn't get up was lying, and people were jumping on him in the middle of the night.
And the horror of the screams.
Families got immediately torn apart.
A child was left there, and the brother was left there, and the husband was left there.
And each one was screaming, one for the other.
And the Germans, with the most vicious, with those German shepherd dogs, that were specially trained to tear you apart.
It was the most horrifying of things.
I'll never forget this.
I mean, I was holding on to my sister.
And finally, I lost her.
And then I finished up with my brother-in-law.
And the people-- that was specially-- let me put it you this way, the panic was indescribable-- indescribable.
The panic of those from two days in a train.
And they forced us to get out of that-- of that-- of those trains.
And then they start screaming that we should line up in lines.
And I remember his face-- Mengele.
And there was like a light, like you see this here.
He was standing behind a light.
And everyone came in front of him.
And he was making with his finger, left and the right.
I would describe him-- I want to tell you something-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] a monster in disguise as a human being.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] His face was like stone dead, like, yellowish, or a thing like that.
And he was standing, and he had such a grin on his face, because every move the one which went to the right, went to Auschwitz, to the camps.
And the left automatically went to the gas chambers.
The gas chambers were already loaded and prepared for them, for those.
That was the horror of things of-- from that man, from there it was horrifying things.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] By the way, my brother-in-law, with me, he was very good looking, went to the right.
And the following day, when we were in Auschwitz, there were numbers tattooed.
We stayed the whole night outside.
They took all our possessions away, and they gave us those-- what do you call it-- those-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a pair of pants and a jacket without pockets, without anything, without socks.
And they took all our-- I remember my mother.
And I was wearing about three suits on me.
And my mother pushed in something, something you can see in that picture.
You will see it is a golden chain, which my mother had it from [INAUDIBLE].
And she put this into each one.
You will need it, maybe, to be able to survive.
And she put it in my jacket, from there it is.
Of course, they took away all the clothes and all the things.
And they gave you those clothes.
I'm sure you have seen it, which has that colored [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] blue and gray.
[INAUDIBLE] and you looked at each other.
You looked like clowns.
Some [INAUDIBLE] we started laughing hysterically.
You couldn't believe it, how you look in those kind of things.
I never saw anything like this.
It was a horrible thing, in that Auschwitz.
And by the way, my brother-in-law was a wonderful human being.
And he was assigned to some other work.
I used to see him a couple of days during-- during work.
And then I found out that he went to the electric wire.
He commit suicide [SIGHING] when he heard that my sister was no more alive.
[PAUSES FOR 17 SECONDS] But yeah, I want to-- to mention another episode which happened in Auschwitz.
I was assigned with another group.
[INAUDIBLE] the-- the-- the Auschwitz itself was the most horrifying of things.
To get up 7 o'clock in the morning and stand in the rain and cold, and they used to count you over and over just to give you a little bit of black water.
And each one was fighting over it, to get a few sips.
[INAUDIBLE] then you worked a whole day.
And I was assigned one particular place, where we were storing certain German's machinery.
I remember where it was.
Of course, at 1 o'clock, in the middle of the day, or maybe 12:30, it was lunchtime.
We had nothing to eat.
It was only made to rest for the German guards.
And this is a terrible, horrifying thing that I have seen, because they had their sandwiches packed neatly and nicely.
They broke you, so you become like an animal.
You didn't think any more for your parents or your loved ones.
You just became with your body hunger.
People don't understand what kind of horrifying death-- there's every day a part of your body dies.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And the rest of them are fighting to survive.
You cannot fight.
You cannot walk.
You cannot work.
You cannot think.
The only one thing is on your mind-- to get a potato, to get a piece of bread.
That's all [INAUDIBLE].
You have turned on an animal instinct.
The only difference was you walked on two feet.
But you were actually an animal.
That's where they succeeded, to degrade you to such a point.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Another soldier got-- put his two feet on that iron part from the other side.
And they put the handle across.
And another one jumped on the handle from the other side.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Now they're about to choke him to death.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I don't know if it was just-- I saw it so vividly there.
And that was not-- [INAUDIBLE] he asked him, [SPEAKING GERMAN].
Do this once more again.
And I remember the blood was coming out from his ears, and from his throat, from jumping.
[INAUDIBLE] It was sort of like you hang somebody.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] while blood was coming out, the German dog rushed over to start licking that blood.
And he started hysterically to laugh that the dog now has Juden blood, that-- that the-- that the dog has become half Jewish.
And he was hysterically laughing at it.
And that's the way, how you met that horrifying death.
That wasn't the end of it.
We had to carry him back after work to Auschwitz.
It was a few miles.
I remember I was holding him in one hand like this, and another one.
And two was holding him by the feet.
And I remember the blood was still coming all over me.
And this is how you met death, by having the audacity trying to grab that piece of skin from a salami.
It was in Auschwitz, which is-- I say this even today.
And I would pick out that German even today.
[INAUDIBLE]
